title: Scabies
oneLiner: Pruritic skin infestation by *Sarcoptes scabiei* mite; highly contagious via skin contact; treat with topical scabicide + treat contacts simultaneously.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Secondary bacterial sepsis** (impetigo/cellulitis → systemic infection) – single finding: **fever + spreading erythema + pus/crusting** (not just excoriations)
- **Crusted (Norwegian) scabies** in immunocompromised – single finding: **hyperkeratotic plaques on hands/feet + widespread scaling with minimal itch** (high mite load → rapid spread + high risk of secondary sepsis)
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you’ll actually see):
  - Intense pruritus, **worse at night** (classic)
  - Burrows: **linear, greyish, slightly raised tracks** (1–10 mm) – most common in interdigital web spaces, wrists, elbows, axillae, areolae, belt line, genitals
  - Papules, vesicles, pustules (secondary to scratching)
  - **History of close contact** with an itchy person (household, sexual partner, care home)
- Key exam findings:
  - Burrow distribution (webs, flexures) – **spares face/scalp in adults** (except infants/elderly)
  - **Nodules** on scrotum/pens (pathognomonic)
  - Excoriations, eczematisation, lichenification (chronic scratching)
  - In infants: palms, soles, scalp may be involved
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely, with one-line differentiators:
  - **Atopic eczema** – no burrows, family history, flexural lichenification, responds to steroids (scabies worsens with steroids)
  - **Contact dermatitis** – localised to allergen exposure, no nocturnal pattern
  - **Insect bite reactions** – grouped lesions, seasonal, no burrows
  - **Papular urticaria** – recurrent crops, child with pets, no interdigital burrows
  - **Drug reaction** – temporal association with new drug, no burrows, often more widespread morbiliform
  - **Dermatophyte infection** – scaling, annular lesions, KOH positive
- **Splitter:** **Burrow identified** (by dermoscopy or ink test) – if present → scabies; if absent → consider eczema/contact dermatitis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Dermoscopy** (burrow: “delta-wing” sign – triangular dark shape at end of burrow)
  - **Burrow ink test** – rub pen ink over suspected track, wipe off; ink retained in burrow shows as a thin dark line
  - **Skin scraping** (if diagnostic doubt) – scrape burrow with scalpel, place on slide with mineral oil, cover slip, microscopy (mites, eggs, scybala)
- **Bloods:**
  - Not routine; check FBC (eosinophilia ~50% of patients), IgE (elevated in chronic infestation)
- **Imaging:** None
- **Special tests:** None routinely needed; polymerase chain reaction (PCR) available in reference labs but rarely required
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** No validated bedside score – **diagnosis is clinical.** Suspect in any patient with nocturnal pruritus + burrows/typical distribution + contact history
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin scraping** in suspected crusted scabies (confirms diagnosis and guides isolation/infection control)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Isolate patient (single room, contact precautions until first treatment completed)
  - Obtain clear history of contacts (household, sexual partners within past 4–6 weeks)
  - Apply first dose of topical scabicide (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Give oral antihistamine (e.g. chlorphenamine 4 mg PO) for itch
  - If secondary infection present: start appropriate antibiotics (flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS PO for 7 days – check local policy for MRSA risk)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Repeat topical scabicide **exactly 7 days later** (7-day interval is critical; do not delay)
  - Treat all close contacts **simultaneously** on the same day (household, sexual partners, care home residents/staff) – even if asymptomatic
  - Provide written advice: wash all bedding/clothes at ≥50°C or seal in plastic bag for 72 hours
  - For crusted scabies: **urgent dermatology referral; combined topical + oral therapy** (e.g. permethrin 5% daily for 7 days + oral ivermectin [check local policy] on days 1, 2, 8, 9, 15); strict barrier nursing; ketoconazole shampoo for scalp
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Review at 2 weeks: reassess itch and lesions; perform a second scrape if symptoms persist
  - If ongoing itch: treat as post-scabies pruritus (topical emollients + oral antihistamine; can last 4–6 weeks)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Isolation:** contact precautions until 24 hours after first treatment
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not routine unless immobile (assess with Caprini/Risk Assessment Tool)
  - **Diet/NBM:** no restrictions
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 as per local protocol (usually 4-hourly if stable)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent (topical):**
  - **Permethrin 5% cream** (e.g. Lyclear)
  - Dose: Apply to whole body from neck down (include scalp in infants/elderly) – leave on for 8–12 hours, then wash off
  - Repeat **one application after 7 days** (to kill newly hatched mites)
- **Alternative (if permethrin intolerance):**
  - **Malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion** (e.g. Derbac-M)
  - Dose: Apply similarly; leave on for 24 hours, wash off; repeat in 7 days
- **Oral therapy (for resistant/crusted scabies – specialist use only):**
  - **Ivermectin** 200 micrograms/kg PO – single dose, repeat in 7 days; for crusted scabies, combined course (see ward management) – [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Permethrin: avoid in raw/weeping skin; may cause transient stinging; safety in pregnancy/lactation – limit to neck-down application
  - Ivermectin: contraindicated in pregnancy, breastfeeding, children <15 kg; caution with warfarin (monitor INR), CNS depressants
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Topical scabicides: no adjustment needed
  - Ivermectin: caution in severe hepatic impairment (may increase neurotoxicity) – [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Permethrin – applying to face/scalp in adults** (unnecessary, may cause irritation; only needed for infants/elderly/scalp involvement)
  - **Ivermectin – using without confirming crusted scabies or specialist advice** (risk of neurological adverse effects; must not be used in pregnancy)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial infection: impetigo, cellulitis, abscess, lymphadenopathy – most common in crusted scabies
  - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (rare, following nephritogenic streptococcal strains)
  - Systemic sepsis (especially crusted scabies with high bacterial load)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-scabies pruritus (persists 2–6 weeks after successful treatment)
  - Eczematous dermatitis (from chronic scratching)
  - Nodular scabies (nodules persist months)
  - Psychological distress (insomnia, social stigma)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Excellent** with correct treatment and contact management – complete resolution within 2 weeks
- **Reinfection** common if contacts not treated simultaneously
- **Crusted scabies** has high morbidity if untreated; mortality up to 50% in institutionalised/severely immunocompromised (mostly due to sepsis)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Treat ALL contacts at the same time** – even those without symptoms (asymptomatic carriers can transmit)
- **Repeat the scabicide exactly 7 days later** – missing this step is the most common cause of treatment failure
- **Give antihistamines for pruritus** – prevents scratching that causes secondary infection and masks response
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not apply steroid creams before confirming diagnosis** – steroids mask burrows and worsen scabies (increased mite survival)
- **Do not assume itch is eczema** – always check burrows and contact history; misdiagnosis delays treatment and spreads infestation
- **Do not forget to treat the scalp in infants/elderly** – topical scabicide must cover the scalp in these groups
- **Do not use oral ivermectin without specialist input** – risk of neurotoxicity; must not be used in pregnancy
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Itch not improving after 2 weeks of treatment completion
  - New burrows or papules appear after treatment (possible reinfection or treatment failure)
  - Signs of secondary infection: spreading redness, swelling, pain, pus, fever
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or dermatology clinic in 2 weeks for reassessment
  - If crusted scabies: dermatology follow-up in 1 week; contact tracing by local health protection team
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / dermatology if:**
  - Suspected crusted scabies (requires combined topical + oral therapy, strict isolation)
  - Widespread secondary infection with systemic signs (fever, tachycardia, raised lactate)
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - Sepsis (criteria: NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to fluid challenge)
  - Anaphylaxis (rare, from topical scabicide – suspect if sudden onset urticaria, stridor, hypotension)

rawMarkdown:
## Scabies
**One-liner:** Pruritic skin infestation by *Sarcoptes scabiei* mite; highly contagious via skin contact; treat with topical scabicide + treat contacts simultaneously.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Secondary bacterial sepsis** (impetigo/cellulitis → systemic infection) – single finding: **fever + spreading erythema + pus/crusting** (not just excoriations)
- **Crusted (Norwegian) scabies** in immunocompromised – single finding: **hyperkeratotic plaques on hands/feet + widespread scaling with minimal itch** (high mite load → rapid spread + high risk of secondary sepsis)

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you’ll actually see):
  - Intense pruritus, **worse at night** (classic)
  - Burrows: **linear, greyish, slightly raised tracks** (1–10 mm) – most common in interdigital web spaces, wrists, elbows, axillae, areolae, belt line, genitals
  - Papules, vesicles, pustules (secondary to scratching)
  - **History of close contact** with an itchy person (household, sexual partner, care home)
- Key exam findings:
  - Burrow distribution (webs, flexures) – **spares face/scalp in adults** (except infants/elderly)
  - **Nodules** on scrotum/pens (pathognomonic)
  - Excoriations, eczematisation, lichenification (chronic scratching)
  - In infants: palms, soles, scalp may be involved

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely → less likely, with one-line differentiators:
  - **Atopic eczema** – no burrows, family history, flexural lichenification, responds to steroids (scabies worsens with steroids)
  - **Contact dermatitis** – localised to allergen exposure, no nocturnal pattern
  - **Insect bite reactions** – grouped lesions, seasonal, no burrows
  - **Papular urticaria** – recurrent crops, child with pets, no interdigital burrows
  - **Drug reaction** – temporal association with new drug, no burrows, often more widespread morbiliform
  - **Dermatophyte infection** – scaling, annular lesions, KOH positive
- **Splitter:** **Burrow identified** (by dermoscopy or ink test) – if present → scabies; if absent → consider eczema/contact dermatitis

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Dermoscopy** (burrow: “delta-wing” sign – triangular dark shape at end of burrow)
  - **Burrow ink test** – rub pen ink over suspected track, wipe off; ink retained in burrow shows as a thin dark line
  - **Skin scraping** (if diagnostic doubt) – scrape burrow with scalpel, place on slide with mineral oil, cover slip, microscopy (mites, eggs, scybala)
- **Bloods:**
  - Not routine; check FBC (eosinophilia ~50% of patients), IgE (elevated in chronic infestation)
- **Imaging:** None
- **Special tests:** None routinely needed; polymerase chain reaction (PCR) available in reference labs but rarely required
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** No validated bedside score – **diagnosis is clinical.** Suspect in any patient with nocturnal pruritus + burrows/typical distribution + contact history
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin scraping** in suspected crusted scabies (confirms diagnosis and guides isolation/infection control)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Isolate patient (single room, contact precautions until first treatment completed)
  - Obtain clear history of contacts (household, sexual partners within past 4–6 weeks)
  - Apply first dose of topical scabicide (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Give oral antihistamine (e.g. chlorphenamine 4 mg PO) for itch
  - If secondary infection present: start appropriate antibiotics (flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS PO for 7 days – check local policy for MRSA risk)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Repeat topical scabicide **exactly 7 days later** (7-day interval is critical; do not delay)
  - Treat all close contacts **simultaneously** on the same day (household, sexual partners, care home residents/staff) – even if asymptomatic
  - Provide written advice: wash all bedding/clothes at ≥50°C or seal in plastic bag for 72 hours
  - For crusted scabies: **urgent dermatology referral; combined topical + oral therapy** (e.g. permethrin 5% daily for 7 days + oral ivermectin [check local policy] on days 1, 2, 8, 9, 15); strict barrier nursing; ketoconazole shampoo for scalp
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Review at 2 weeks: reassess itch and lesions; perform a second scrape if symptoms persist
  - If ongoing itch: treat as post-scabies pruritus (topical emollients + oral antihistamine; can last 4–6 weeks)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Isolation:** contact precautions until 24 hours after first treatment
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not routine unless immobile (assess with Caprini/Risk Assessment Tool)
  - **Diet/NBM:** no restrictions
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 as per local protocol (usually 4-hourly if stable)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent (topical):**
  - **Permethrin 5% cream** (e.g. Lyclear)
  - Dose: Apply to whole body from neck down (include scalp in infants/elderly) – leave on for 8–12 hours, then wash off
  - Repeat **one application after 7 days** (to kill newly hatched mites)
- **Alternative (if permethrin intolerance):**
  - **Malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion** (e.g. Derbac-M)
  - Dose: Apply similarly; leave on for 24 hours, wash off; repeat in 7 days
- **Oral therapy (for resistant/crusted scabies – specialist use only):**
  - **Ivermectin** 200 micrograms/kg PO – single dose, repeat in 7 days; for crusted scabies, combined course (see ward management) – [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Permethrin: avoid in raw/weeping skin; may cause transient stinging; safety in pregnancy/lactation – limit to neck-down application
  - Ivermectin: contraindicated in pregnancy, breastfeeding, children <15 kg; caution with warfarin (monitor INR), CNS depressants
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Topical scabicides: no adjustment needed
  - Ivermectin: caution in severe hepatic impairment (may increase neurotoxicity) – [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Permethrin – applying to face/scalp in adults** (unnecessary, may cause irritation; only needed for infants/elderly/scalp involvement)
  - **Ivermectin – using without confirming crusted scabies or specialist advice** (risk of neurological adverse effects; must not be used in pregnancy)

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial infection: impetigo, cellulitis, abscess, lymphadenopathy – most common in crusted scabies
  - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (rare, following nephritogenic streptococcal strains)
  - Systemic sepsis (especially crusted scabies with high bacterial load)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-scabies pruritus (persists 2–6 weeks after successful treatment)
  - Eczematous dermatitis (from chronic scratching)
  - Nodular scabies (nodules persist months)
  - Psychological distress (insomnia, social stigma)

### Prognosis
- **Excellent** with correct treatment and contact management – complete resolution within 2 weeks
- **Reinfection** common if contacts not treated simultaneously
- **Crusted scabies** has high morbidity if untreated; mortality up to 50% in institutionalised/severely immunocompromised (mostly due to sepsis)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Treat ALL contacts at the same time** – even those without symptoms (asymptomatic carriers can transmit)
- **Repeat the scabicide exactly 7 days later** – missing this step is the most common cause of treatment failure
- **Give antihistamines for pruritus** – prevents scratching that causes secondary infection and masks response

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not apply steroid creams before confirming diagnosis** – steroids mask burrows and worsen scabies (increased mite survival)
- **Do not assume itch is eczema** – always check burrows and contact history; misdiagnosis delays treatment and spreads infestation
- **Do not forget to treat the scalp in infants/elderly** – topical scabicide must cover the scalp in these groups
- **Do not use oral ivermectin without specialist input** – risk of neurotoxicity; must not be used in pregnancy

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Itch not improving after 2 weeks of treatment completion
  - New burrows or papules appear after treatment (possible reinfection or treatment failure)
  - Signs of secondary infection: spreading redness, swelling, pain, pus, fever
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or dermatology clinic in 2 weeks for reassessment
  - If crusted scabies: dermatology follow-up in 1 week; contact tracing by local health protection team

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / dermatology if:**
  - Suspected crusted scabies (requires combined topical + oral therapy, strict isolation)
  - Widespread secondary infection with systemic signs (fever, tachycardia, raised lactate)
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - Sepsis (criteria: NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to fluid challenge)
  - Anaphylaxis (rare, from topical scabicide – suspect if sudden onset urticaria, stridor, hypotension)
